meta.note: DEMO question set for PEFEPrep. Replaces with the Handbook-verified bank generated by the daily FE engine (FE_Question_Bank.xlsx / questions.json).

questions.stem: Water flows through a circular pipe of inside diameter 0.10 m at an average velocity of 2.0 m/s. What is the volumetric flow rate Q?
Water (kinematic viscosity ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows at 1.5 m/s through a 0.05 m diameter pipe. What is the Reynolds number?
Per the NCEES FE Reference Handbook, flow in a circular pipe is considered fully turbulent when the Reynolds number exceeds approximately:
questions.solution: The FE Handbook defines laminar flow for Re < 2,100 and fully turbulent flow for Re > 10,000 (transitional in between). Note: the common textbook value of 4,000 is NOT the Handbook threshold — use 10,000.
questions.handbook: Fluid Mechanics — Reynolds Number / Flow Regimes

questions.stem: A 100 m pipe (D = 0.10 m) carries water at 2.0 m/s with a Darcy friction factor f = 0.02. Using g = 9.81 m/s², the head loss due to friction is closest to:

questions.concept: Minor-loss notation (Handbook uses C, not K)
questions.stem: The FE Handbook expresses the head loss across a fitting as h_f = C·(V²/2g). In this expression, C is the:
questions.solution: In the Handbook the minor (fitting) loss is h_f,fitting = C·(V²/2g), where C is the loss coefficient. (Many textbooks call this K — but match the Handbook's notation, C, on the exam.)
questions.handbook: Fluid Mechanics — Minor Losses in Pipe Fittings

questions.concept: Hydraulic radius of a full circular pipe
questions.stem: For a circular pipe flowing full, the hydraulic radius R (= cross-sectional area / wetted perimeter) equals:
questions.solution: R = A/P = (πD²/4)/(πD) = D/4. (Do not confuse with hydraulic diameter D_H = 4A/P = D, which differs by a factor of 4.)
